role_summary: SNCC voter-registration worker in McComb, Greenwood, and Hattiesburg, Mississippi; arrested with Fannie Lou Hamer at the Winona bus station in June 1963; Tougaloo College student after the Voting Rights Act.
ai_reading: Simpson's testimony sits with a tight cluster of Mississippi voter-registration colleagues in the embedding. Her top six semantic neighbors include Charles McLaurin (Sunflower County project director, entry 17), Frankye Adams-Johnson (entry 43), the joint Ladner sisters interview (entry 75), and Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96), all Mississippi-side SNCC organizers; the embedding therefore confirms her cultural placement inside the Mississippi project network. Her position on the Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle concept axis is +0.47, far toward the Southern pole, the second-most-Southern reading among the audited interviewees built so far in this catalog and consistent with her geography of work (McComb, Greenwood, Hattiesburg, Jackson). Where the embedding offers something the cultural record alone would not have made obvious is the comparatively secular framing the vector picks up: at +0.40 along Sacred Framing vs Secular Framing, Simpson's testimony reads more toward the secular-political end than the Mississippi voter-registration scene's heavily Black-Baptist context would predict, possibly an artifact of the singing-as-coping memory dominating the religious vocabulary in her transcript.
biographical_paragraph: Simpson was seventeen when she joined SNCC in 1962, the conversation that approved her involvement having taken place between her father and SNCC field secretary Lawrence Guyot [src: 1]. Itta Bena, Mississippi was where she was raised; she was born December 12, 1945 [src: 1]. From the Greenwood Council of Federated Organizations office she did voter-registration work that ran through McComb, Greenwood, and Hattiesburg, and during Freedom Summer in 1964 she worked from the Jackson COFO office and helped coordinate the Freedom Vote campaign [src: 1]. The episode that fixed her in the documentary record came in June 1963, when she, Fannie Lou Hamer, June Johnson, and Annell Ponder were arrested at a bus station in Winona, Mississippi; while incarcerated, Simpson tended to Hamer after the beating Hamer suffered in custody, applying cold towels to Hamer's forehead [src: 1]. She used singing in jail as a way through [src: 1]. After the Voting Rights Act passed she enrolled at Tougaloo College in 1965 with scholarship funding SNCC helped arrange [src: 1]. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 41 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 2].
movement_context: The June 9, 1963 Winona, Mississippi Greyhound bus station incident was the precipitating event for the most heavily-documented Black Belt jail beating of the 1962-1965 SNCC Mississippi voter-registration period [src: 3]. Simpson, Fannie Lou Hamer, Annell Ponder, June Johnson, Lawrence Guyot, and James West were returning by Greyhound from a Septima Clark / SCLC citizenship-school training session in Charleston, South Carolina when the bus stopped at the Winona depot in Montgomery County; the group attempted to integrate the white waiting room and the lunch counter, and were arrested by Montgomery County Sheriff Earle Wayne Patridge, Police Chief Thomas Herod Jr., and Mississippi Highway Patrolman John L. Basinger [src: 3]. Inside the Montgomery County jail Ponder, Johnson, and Hamer were beaten by the arresting officers and by two Black prisoners coerced to administer the blows under threat; Simpson, who was sixteen at the time, was placed in the cell with Hamer and applied cold towels through the night while Hamer recited Psalms and the group sang freedom songs through the cell bars [src: 3]. The injuries Hamer sustained (kidney damage, a permanent limp, partial blindness in her left eye) would shape the rest of her life [src: 4]. The August 22, 1964 Hamer testimony before the Democratic National Convention Credentials Committee in Atlantic City was built around the Winona beating Simpson had witnessed and tended through; Hamer's eight-minute account of the jail violence broke into national prime-time programming when President Lyndon Johnson called a hastily-arranged White House press conference to displace it, and the three television networks aired the testimony in full on their late-evening news programs [src: 4]. The SNCC Mississippi field-staff infrastructure Simpson worked inside (the McComb, Greenwood, Hattiesburg, and Jackson COFO offices) was the same organizing network Charles McLaurin (catalog entry 17) had opened in Sunflower County in August 1962; Simpson's Greenwood and Sunflower County voter-registration field work between 1962 and 1965 ran through the same circuit of mass meetings, citizenship classes, and courthouse delegations Bob Moses, McLaurin, Hollis Watkins, and Curtis Hayes had built into the Mississippi project [src: 5].
legacy_and_reception: Simpson's first-person account of the Winona jail beating is the principal eyewitness contribution to the collective-memoir record of the incident, anchoring the relevant chapter in Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, Judy Richardson, Betty Garman Robinson, Jean Smith Young, and Dorothy M. Zellner's edited 2010 University of Illinois Press volume Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC (paperback New Press 2014), which Dottie Zellner (catalog entry 30) co-edited [src: 6]. The 2014 SNCC 50th Anniversary Conference at Shaw University in Raleigh that Courtland Cox (catalog entry 24) anchored brought Simpson back into public-facing SNCC alumni programming alongside the Mississippi-project cohort that included Hollis Watkins, Lawrence Guyot, and Charles McLaurin; the conference proceedings and the post-conference SNCC Legacy Project archive at the Robert W. Woodruff Library at the Atlanta University Center include her recorded remarks on the Winona incident and on the Greenwood organizing years [src: 7]. Simpson has continued to live and work in the Mississippi Delta region, with extended periods of residence in Greenwood and the surrounding Leflore and Sunflower County area; her continuing role as a SNCC alumni network speaker has included voter-engagement events at Tougaloo College (her undergraduate institution, where she enrolled in 1965 with SNCC-arranged scholarship support) and at the Fannie Lou Hamer Day commemoration the Sunflower County Civil Rights Organization holds each October 6 in Ruleville [src: 7].

gallery.alt: Fannie Lou Hamer at the Democratic National Convention, Atlantic City, August 22, 1964
gallery.caption: Fannie Lou Hamer at the Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City, New Jersey, August 22, 1964, the day she testified before the Credentials Committee about the June 9, 1963 beating in the Montgomery County jail in Winona, Mississippi that Simpson had tended her through. Simpson, sixteen years old at the time of the Winona arrest, was placed in Hamer's cell and applied cold towels through the night as Hamer recovered from the kidney damage and partial blindness the beating produced. Photographed by Warren K. Leffler for U.S. News & World Report.
gallery.photographer: Warren K. Leffler (U.S. News & World Report)
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Library of Congress, U.S. News & World Report collection, digital ID ds.07134)
gallery.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: no known copyright restrictions; U.S. News & World Report dedicated all rights to the public upon donation)

gallery.alt: Tougaloo College Civil Rights Marker, Jackson, Mississippi
gallery.caption: The Tougaloo College Civil Rights Marker on the historically Black college campus in Jackson, Mississippi where Simpson enrolled in 1965 with SNCC-arranged scholarship funding after the Voting Rights Act passed. Tougaloo was the institutional base for the Jackson COFO office Simpson worked from during Freedom Summer 1964 and through the Freedom Vote campaign, and continued as the academic home for many SNCC veterans transitioning from full-time field organizing to undergraduate study after 1965.

gallery.alt: Sunflower County Courthouse in Indianola, Mississippi
gallery.caption: The Sunflower County Courthouse in Indianola, Mississippi. Simpson's Greenwood and Delta-region voter-registration field work between 1962 and 1965 ran through the same SNCC Mississippi project network that Charles McLaurin (catalog entry 17) opened at this courthouse in August 1962, and the same network that produced the Hamer, Ponder, Johnson, Guyot, and West delegation arrested at the Winona bus station on June 9, 1963.

sources.title: Euvester Simpson
sources.publisher: SNCC Digital Gateway, Duke University (in collaboration with the SNCC Legacy Project; working from the SNCC papers at the Robert W. Woodruff Library, Atlanta University Center)

sources.title: Euvester Simpson oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Kay Mills, This Little Light of Mine: The Life of Fannie Lou Hamer
sources.publisher: Plume / Penguin, 1994 (second edition University Press of Kentucky, 2007); WorldCat OCLC 29521716 (the standard biography of Hamer; chapters 6 and 7 document the June 9, 1963 Winona bus station incident, the Montgomery County jail beating, and Simpson's eyewitness presence with Hamer through the night)

sources.title: Maegan Parker Brooks, A Voice That Could Stir an Army: Fannie Lou Hamer and the Rhetoric of the Black Freedom Movement
sources.publisher: University Press of Mississippi, 2014; WorldCat OCLC 856849317 (the principal rhetorical-scholarship account of the August 22, 1964 Hamer testimony before the Democratic National Convention Credentials Committee, including the Winona-beating narrative architecture)

sources.title: Charles M. Payne, I've Got the Light of Freedom: The Organizing Tradition and the Mississippi Freedom Struggle
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 1995 (second edition 2007); WorldCat OCLC 61664854 (the standard institutional history of SNCC's Mississippi voter-registration project, including the Greenwood, McComb, Hattiesburg, and Sunflower County circuits Simpson worked through)

sources.title: Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, Judy Richardson, Betty Garman Robinson, Jean Smith Young, and Dorothy M. Zellner, eds., Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC
sources.publisher: University of Illinois Press, 2010 (paperback New Press 2014); WorldCat OCLC 635021942 (the principal collective-memoir archival document of the SNCC women's cohort; Simpson's first-person account of the Winona jail beating is included)

sources.title: Wesley C. Hogan, Many Minds, One Heart: SNCC's Dream for a New America
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 2007 (paperback 2009); WorldCat OCLC 192129687 (institutional history of SNCC documenting the 2014 50th Anniversary Conference at Shaw University and the SNCC Legacy Project archive)

sources.title: Hasan Kwame Jeffries, Bloody Lowndes: Civil Rights and Black Power in Alabama's Black Belt
sources.publisher: New York University Press, 2009; WorldCat OCLC 319497555 (the Black Belt Southern voter-registration institutional history that situates the SNCC Mississippi project Simpson worked alongside the parallel Alabama Black Belt organizing)
